text: Women missionaries in the 19th century received more public acceptance and career opportunities abroad than they did at home. As pioneers of evangelical and social reforms through evangelism, education and medicine in various parts of Asia and Africa, their evangelising methods and motivations have been the subject of increasing critique. This article calls for a re-appraisal of these women missionaries and their self-sacrificing devotion to their work, acknowledging their role as catalysts of what is arguably the most significant period of Christian expansion in history. These women who crossed the comfort of their homes to toil in foreign lands and make a lasting impact on the lands to which they travelled, exemplified in their lives the Pauline principle of striving for higher things, investing in eternal values while eschewing earthly gain.
